title: Optical nonlinear anomalous Hall effect reveals the hidden spin order in antiferromagnets
authors: A. Schmid, D. Siebenkotten, D. Dai, J. Godinho, T. Ostatnický, N. Zou, Y. Zhang, J. Železný, Z. Šobáň, F. Křížek, V. Novák, S. Fairman, A. Hoehl, A. Hertwig, T. Janda, M. A. Huber, R. Huber, B. Kästner, J. Wunderlich
abstract: Reading antiferromagnetic order remains a central obstacle for antiferromagnetic memory and logic because zero net magnetisation precludes conventional magnetic readout. Domain imaging typically relies on x-ray magnetic linear dichroism (XMLD) microscopy at synchrotron sources, but XMLD is even under time reversal and cannot distinguish 180°-reversed magnetic states. Here we report the first experimental observation of the optical nonlinear anomalous Hall effect, predicted for antiferromagnets with combined parity - time-reversal ($PT$) symmetry. The effect stems from light-induced interband electric-dipole transitions, where spin-orbit coupling induces an asymmetry between $\pm k$ states and generates a time-reversal-odd photocurrent whose sign flips upon 180° reversal of the Néel vector. In $PT$-symmetric CuMnAs, we use near-field excitation to map this photocurrent with sub-100-nm spatial resolution after current-induced spin-orbit-torque switching. The signal polarity follows local Néel vector reversal, enabling nanoscale imaging of antiferromagnetic texture and direct readout of 180°-reversed antiferromagnetic states that remain indistinguishable in XMLD and other time-reversal-even linear-dichroic probes. The optical nonlinear anomalous Hall effect thus reveals a new light-spin interaction and provides a scalable route to nanoscale readout of hidden spin order, with potential for ultrafast all-electrical and all-optical antiferromagnetic spintronic technologies.
summary: This paper reports the discovery of the optical nonlinear anomalous Hall effect as a way to 'see' hidden spin order in antiferromagnets. By using mid-infrared near-field microscopy, the authors can distinguish between magnetic domains that appear identical to standard X-ray techniques. This provides a scalable, nanoscale method for reading out antiferromagnetic states. Such a breakthrough is crucial for developing next-generation, ultrafast antiferromagnetic spintronic memory and logic devices.
main_problem: Developing a nanoscale readout method for antiferromagnets that can distinguish between 180-degree reversed Néel domains, which are indistinguishable to conventional time-reversal-even probes like XMLD.
main_result: The first experimental observation of the optical nonlinear anomalous Hall effect (oNAHE) in PT-symmetric CuMnAs, enabling sub-100-nm imaging of the Néel vector orientation.
method: The researchers used scattering-type scanning near-field optical microscopy (s-SNOM) with mid-infrared excitation and combined it with current-induced spin-orbit-torque switching to manipulate and read out magnetic domains.
model_or_system: The study focuses on PT-symmetric antiferromagnetic thin films of CuMnAs. The physical mechanism involves a second-order nonlinear optical response driven by interband electric-dipole transitions governed by the quantum metric tensor.
key_observables: Optical nonlinear anomalous Hall voltage (V_oNA), which is time-reversal-odd and changes sign upon 180-degree reversal of the Néel vector.
important_parameters: Mid-infrared wavelength (10 um), sub-100 nm spatial resolution, and CuMnAs film thickness of approximately 20 nm.
main_assumptions: The simulations assume plane-wave excitation and a diffraction-limited focusing of incident power; the s-SNOM technique is noted to have significant background contributions from the large focal spot size.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the s-SNOM setup and a color-coded voltage map of AFM domains. Figure 3 presents DFT-calculated band dispersion and simulated voltage maps. Figure 4 demonstrates the experimental write-read protocol in crossbar devices.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the readout challenge in antiferromagnetic spintronics, describes the experimental s-SNOM platform and the oNAHE mechanism, presents experimental domain mapping and electrical switching results, and validates the findings with DFT calculations and near-field simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The strongest polarity‑dependent changes occur at the current‑pulse‑affected corners (dashed outlines).
